Now, let's go back for a second to that magic moment many years ago when I invented the straight line. And I was in front of my room of guys, 12, 13 rag tag salespeople, and I gave them that, you know, that great speed. I said, don't you get it? Every sales the same. And I went to a board that said, watch, there's an open and there's a close. Now, as you move someone down the straight line, it's goal-oriented communication. You're pushing them from the open to the close. And what I said to them was, if in those first four seconds that you speak to someone, the people on the other end of the line don't perceive that you're number one, sharp as attack, number two, enthusiastic as hell, three, an expert in your field, then they will tune out. You're done. Now in truth, you have 10 or 15 seconds to play catch a ball. They could think that you don't really have what it takes in the beginning. But if in those first four seconds, 10 seconds after, they don't believe you're sharp, enthusiastic, an expert in your field, very, very difficult to influence them. And in those first few seconds to be perceived, sharp as attack, enthusiastic as hell is an expert in your field. Those three things chunk up to where the other person says, hmm, he's probably a person worth listening to. And that's why they listen. That's why they'll even listen to what you have to say. And then if you want to even chunk that to a higher level still, they're worth listening to because they can help me achieve my goals. They can help me get what I want. The one great mistake that novice salespeople make is they lose sight very often that the reason people are listening to them is not to get what they want. I mean, the salesperson wants it because the people listening because they want to get something that they want, they have a need to fill. That's the only reason they're not hanging up the phone or walking out there. They're trying to fill their old need. And when they look at you as a person who could potentially give that to them, they tend to listen. That's, something happens very first four, five seconds. Maybe by 15 to 20 seconds, it's sort of lockstep. It's either there or it's not. You can't play catch-up anymore. And if they don't perceive you right away in those first four seconds that your number one sharp is a tap. Now notice here, this is a behavioral competency to be sharp on the ball. They don't perceive that instantly. They say, ah, you know, why should I listen to this person? They just tune out. Next that you are enthusiastic as hell. Remember enthusiasm, another behavioral competency. They say, wow, what's this guy have to talk about? Why is it so good? It must be good if he's that enthusiastic. They can't help but want to listen more. And three, which is authoritative, another competency, an expert in your field. Bam. When these three things are present right in those first few seconds, what they say is, hmm, maybe I should listen to this person. Maybe this is that person that could help me get what I want. Now you say to yourself, old Jordan. Okay, that makes a lot of sense. Yeah, I mean, if someone thinks I'm really sharp on the ball, I'm an expert and, you know, I'm really enthusiastic, they'll say, yeah, of course, listen to this guy, right? But how could they possibly know that in four seconds flat? It can't be because of some magic words I say. You can't say, listen to me, I'm sharp, you can't do that, right? It takes kind of a long time through words to get that sort of point across to somebody. So if it's not the words that establish that, guess what it is? It's your tonality. It's that ability that in that first pop out of the phone or in person or the way you dress when they lay eyes on you that bam, something registers unconsciously that they just say to themselves, wow, this is a person worth listening to. Now conversely, let's go the other way. Let's say you pick up the phone, you doll, you don't sound the right way and you sound unenthusiastic, you sound like a dullard and you know it's not like you're an expert, like a novice in your field, what do people do? They hang up the phone. Makes sense? Remember, sharp as a tack, enthusiastic as hell, an expert in your field. And now let's take that to some real pure science and behavioral competencies here in terms of this communication. 9% words, 50% tonality, 40% body language, 41% body language. Here's the question. If you're on the phone talking to someone, can they see you? There's no body language, right? They can't see you. It's just a voice over the phone and the words you say, can they see you? You're probably saying, well, no, there's no way they can see you. The answer is yes, they can. You see, whether or not they can actually see you in person, they will make a mental image of you based on how you sound and what you say, that mental image serves this higher purpose, which is that you are a person worth listening to, or a very bad place to be, which is, this is someone I should really hang up on because they don't know what the hell they're talking about. And that comes across, believe it or not, in four seconds on the phone and in person, it's a quarter of a second. They've actually tested this out, our psychologists scientifically, where they hook you up to a functional magnetic resonance imaging technology and they flash a picture in front of someone and bam, the brain spikes up and down in a quarter of a second to the judgment center. And our brain judges based on what they see, whether it's good, bad, sharp, not sharp, and makes a decision accordingly. So if that's so important, that mental image is so important, those words are so crucial, what do you do? How do you get to that point, whether you're on the phone or in person, that no matter what happens, no matter how you're feeling inside, that you come off the right way, which is sharp as a tack, enthusiastic as hell, and an expert in your field. When you can get to that point, you've really begun to master the art of tonality.
